response:
the jamul indian village community center shaped as a regular hexagon with sides of thirty meters yields an area derived by dividing the polygon into six equilateral triangles where each triangle has a base and height determined through trigonometric identities involving sine and cosine functions resulting in a total hexagonal area of approximately two thousand six hundred fifty square meters calculated using the formula for a regular hexagon which is three times the square root of three divided by two multiplied by the side length squared.


the circular garden situated at the center touches the midpoints of the hexagon sides meaning its radius equals the apothem of the hexagon which is found by multiplying the side length by the square root of three divided by two giving a radius of approximately twenty five point nine eight meters and the area of this circle is computed by squaring this radius and multiplying by pi to yield roughly two thousand one hundred twenty one square meters demonstrating how geometric principles integrate the hexagonal structure with the central circular design.
